title: Plugin LLM Access
summary: Documents ctx.llm, the API for plugins to make out-of-band LLM calls using the host's credentials, covering complete(), complete_structured(), async variants (acomplete/acomplete_structured), structured output with JSON schema, trust gate configuration, and result attributes.

config_keys.description: Allow plugin to specify a different provider
Allow plugin to specify a different model
Allowlist of providers the plugin may use
Allowlist of models the plugin may use

code_examples.caption: Minimal LLM call from a plugin
Structured output with JSON schema
code_examples.code:
result = ctx.llm.complete_structured(
    instructions="Score this support reply for urgency (0-1).",
    input=[{"type": "text", "text": message_body}],
    json_schema=TRIAGE_SCHEMA,
    purpose="support.triage",
    temperature=0.0,
    max_tokens=128,
)
if result.parsed["urgency"] > 0.8:
    await dispatch_to_oncall(result.parsed["category"], message_body)

code_examples.caption: Async variant
code_examples.code:
result = await ctx.llm.acomplete(messages=...)
result = await ctx.llm.acomplete_structured(instructions=..., input=...)

gotchas: Default posture is fail-closed â€” provider= and model= arguments raise PluginLlmTrustError without operator opt-in
Granting allow_model_override does NOT also grant allow_provider_override â€” each is independently gated
Every call runs against the user's paid provider â€” don't loop on complete() for every gateway message without considering token spend
complete_structured() raises ValueError on empty inputs and schema-validation failure
result.content_type == 'text' means parsing or validation failed â€” inspect result.text for raw response
full_text:
# Plugin LLM Access

`ctx.llm` is the supported way for a plugin to make an LLM call. Chat completion, structured extraction, sync, async, with or without images â€” same surface, same trust gate, same host-owned credentials.

Plugins reach for this when they need to do something that involves the model but isn't part of the agent's conversation. A hook that rewrites a tool error, a gateway adapter that translates an inbound message, a slash command that summarises a long paste, a scheduled job that scores yesterday's activity.

## The smallest possible call

```python
result = ctx.llm.complete(messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}])
return result.text
```

No keys, no provider config, no SDK initialisation. The plugin runs against whatever provider and model the user is currently using.

## Structured output

```python
result = ctx.llm.complete_structured(
    instructions="Score this support reply for urgency (0-1) and pick a category.",
    input=[{"type": "text", "text": message_body}],
    json_schema=TRIAGE_SCHEMA,
    purpose="support.triage",
    temperature=0.0,
    max_tokens=128,
)

if result.parsed["urgency"] > 0.8:
    await dispatch_to_oncall(result.parsed["category"], message_body)
```

The host requests JSON output from the provider, parses it locally as a fallback, validates against your schema if `jsonschema` is installed, and hands back a Python object on `result.parsed`.

## What this lane gives you

- **One call, four shapes.** `complete()` for chat, `complete_structured()` for typed JSON, `acomplete()` and `acomplete_structured()` for asyncio.
- **Host-owned credentials.** OAuth tokens, refresh flows, the credential pool â€” every credential concept Hermes already has applies. The plugin never sees a token.
- **Bounded.** Single sync or async call. No streaming, no tool loops, no conversation state to manage.
- **Fail-closed trust.** A plugin you've never configured cannot pick its own provider, model, agent, or stored credential.

## API surface

`ctx.llm` is an instance of `agent.plugin_llm.PluginLlm`.

### `complete()`

```python
result = ctx.llm.complete(
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hi"}],
    provider=None,         # optional, gated
    model=None,            # optional, gated
    temperature=None,
    max_tokens=None,
    timeout=None,
    agent_id=None,
    profile=None,
    purpose="optional-audit-string",
)
```

`provider=` and `model=` without operator opt-in raises `PluginLlmTrustError`.

### `complete_structured()`

```python
result = ctx.llm.complete_structured(
    instructions="What you want extracted.",
    input=[
        {"type": "text",  "text": "..."},
        {"type": "image", "data": b"...", "mime_type": "image/png"},
        {"type": "image", "url":  "https://..."},
    ],
    json_schema={...},     # optional â€” triggers parsed result + validation
    json_mode=False,
    schema_name=None,
    system_prompt=None,
    provider=None,
    model=None,
    temperature=None,
    max_tokens=None,
    timeout=None,
)
```

When `json_schema` or `json_mode=True` is supplied, the host requests JSON output, parses locally as a fallback, and validates against your schema.

### Async

```python
result = await ctx.llm.acomplete(messages=...)
result = await ctx.llm.acomplete_structured(instructions=..., input=...)
```

### Result attributes

- `text` â€” the assistant's response
- `provider` â€” e.g. 'openrouter', 'anthropic'
- `model` â€” whatever the provider returned for this call
- `agent_id` â€” whose model/auth was used
- `usage` â€” tokens + cache + cost estimate (input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens, cache_read_tokens, cache_write_tokens, cost_usd)
- `audit` â€” plugin_id, purpose, profile
- `parsed` â€” JSON object when content_type == 'json' (structured only)
- `content_type` â€” 'json' or 'text' (structured only)

## Trust gate

The default behaviour is fail-closed. A plugin can call any of the four methods against the user's active provider and model, and set request-shaping arguments. `provider=`, `model=`, `agent_id=`, and `profile=` raise `PluginLlmTrustError` until the operator opts in via `config.yaml`:

```yaml
plugins:
  entries:
    my-plugin:
      llm:
        allow_provider_override: true
        allowed_providers:
          - openrouter
          - anthropic
        allow_model_override: true
        allowed_models:
          - openai/gpt-4o-mini
        allow_agent_id_override: false
        allow_profile_override: false
```

## What the host owns

- Provider resolution (reads `model.provider` + `model.model` from config)
- Auth (pulls API keys, OAuth tokens from `~/.hermes/auth.json` / env)
- Vision routing (falls back to configured vision model when active text model is text-only)
- Fallback chain (if primary provider 5xxs or 429s)
- Timeout (honours your `timeout=` argument)
- JSON shaping (sends `response_format` to the provider when you ask for JSON)
- Schema validation (validates against your `json_schema` when `jsonschema` is installed)
- Audit log (each call writes one INFO line to `agent.log`)

## When to use which

| You want... | Reach for |
|---|---|
| A free-form text response | `complete()` |
| A typed dict back, validated against a schema | `complete_structured()` |
| Image-or-text input with a typed dict back | `complete_structured()` |
| The same call from async code | `acomplete()` / `acomplete_structured()` |

## Where this fits in the plugin surface

| `ctx.register_tool` | adds a tool the agent can call |
| `ctx.register_platform` | wires a new gateway adapter |
| `ctx.register_image_gen_provider` | replaces an image-gen backend |
| `ctx.register_memory_provider` | replaces the memory backend |
| `ctx.register_context_engine` | replaces the context compressor |
| `ctx.register_hook` | observes a lifecycle event |
| `ctx.llm` | makes its own model call, out of band, without any of the above |

## Reference

- Implementation: `agent/plugin_llm.py`
- Tests: `tests/agent/test_plugin_llm.py`
- Reference plugins: `hermes-example-plugins` companion repo
